channel.title: Field Notes
channel.description: Dispatches from the rocky shore. A working blog by a marine biology graduate student in Tacoma.

item.title: The mussel bed is thinning
item.description: Across one summer and three sites, mean mussel cover fell from sixty one percent at the coolest visit to forty four at the warmest. A small change, the kind you only catch by counting the same rock.

item.title: A reading list for the rainy months
item.description: Nature writing to get me through deadline season, from Rachel Carson to a novel my brother keeps asking about. What is on the desk and what is on the phone.

item.title: Why I stayed in the Pacific Northwest
item.description: Leaving would have sounded braver. But the ocean here has moods, the mountains orient you, and the questions I care about are all within a tide window of home.

item.title: Counting the same rock twice
item.description: The whole method rests on doing a boring thing identically, season after season. The same five meters, the same grid, the same wet kneeling. Here is why the tedium is the point.

item.title: What a warm tide pool feels like
item.description: Mid afternoon, a low tide, the rock holding the day's heat. You can feel why the animals there are running out of room at the top of their range, long before any thermometer tells you.

item.title: NMDS, explained without the jargon
item.description: An ordination is just a way of putting hundreds of species counts on a flat map so similar places land near each other. A short walk through how I read mine.
